frameworks.name: working memory influence on perception
frameworks.one_line: WM contents bias subsequent perceptual judgments of moving stimuli

frameworks.name: motion repulsion effect
frameworks.one_line: Classic angular repulsion tested under multi-feature WM load

core_question: Does multi-feature information held in working memory influence perceptual decision-making, and does a task-irrelevant feature modulate this interaction?
key_assumptions: WM and perception share representational resources
RDK orientation, speed, and density can be independently encoded
manipulations: Shared vs differing features (orientation, speed, density) between RDK1 and RDK3
RDK2 speed varied across three experiments as task-irrelevant feature
key_findings: Motion repulsion effect observed at 30° and 60° angular separations
Effect persisted despite increased multi-feature cognitive load
Task-irrelevant motion speed modulated WM representations
interpretations: Multi-feature WM content shapes perception even when features are task-irrelevant
WM-perception interactions extend beyond single-feature memorization
